name.text: M4Q01: In spatial analysis, Queen contiguity defines two polygons as neighbors if they:

questiontext.text: In spatial analysis, Queen contiguity defines two polygons as neighbors if they:

generalfeedback.text: Queen contiguity (named after the chess queen’s movement) considers polygons as neighbors if they share any boundary point — including corners. Rook contiguity is more restrictive, requiring a shared edge. Queen is more commonly used in public health analyses.

answer.text: Share any boundary point (edge or vertex)

feedback.text: Correct! Queen contiguity (named after the chess queen’s movement) considers polygons as neighbors if they share any boundary point — including corners. Rook contiguity is more restrictive, requiring a shared edge. Queen is more commonly used in public health analyses.

answer.text: Share only a common edge (not just a corner)

feedback.text: Incorrect. Queen contiguity (named after the chess queen’s movement) considers polygons as neighbors if they share any boundary point — including corners. Rook contiguity is more restrictive, requiring a shared edge. Queen is more commonly used in public health analyses.

answer.text: Are within a specified distance of each other

feedback.text: Incorrect. Queen contiguity (named after the chess queen’s movement) considers polygons as neighbors if they share any boundary point — including corners. Rook contiguity is more restrictive, requiring a shared edge. Queen is more commonly used in public health analyses.

answer.text: Have the same attribute value

feedback.text: Incorrect. Queen contiguity (named after the chess queen’s movement) considers polygons as neighbors if they share any boundary point — including corners. Rook contiguity is more restrictive, requiring a shared edge. Queen is more commonly used in public health analyses.

name.text: M4Q02: The function spdep::poly2nb() creates a:

generalfeedback.text: poly2nb() constructs a neighbors list from polygon geometries. Each polygon gets a list of its neighbors’ indices. This neighbor structure is the foundation for spatial weights, which are required for all spatial autocorrelation analyses.

answer.text: Neighbor list identifying which polygons are adjacent to each other

feedback.text: Correct! poly2nb() constructs a neighbors list from polygon geometries. Each polygon gets a list of its neighbors’ indices. This neighbor structure is the foundation for spatial weights, which are required for all spatial autocorrelation analyses.

answer.text: New set of polygon boundaries from point data

feedback.text: Incorrect. poly2nb() constructs a neighbors list from polygon geometries. Each polygon gets a list of its neighbors’ indices. This neighbor structure is the foundation for spatial weights, which are required for all spatial autocorrelation analyses.

answer.text: Statistical model for spatial regression

feedback.text: Incorrect. poly2nb() constructs a neighbors list from polygon geometries. Each polygon gets a list of its neighbors’ indices. This neighbor structure is the foundation for spatial weights, which are required for all spatial autocorrelation analyses.

answer.text: Raster representation of polygon data

feedback.text: Incorrect. poly2nb() constructs a neighbors list from polygon geometries. Each polygon gets a list of its neighbors’ indices. This neighbor structure is the foundation for spatial weights, which are required for all spatial autocorrelation analyses.

name.text: M4Q03: Spatial weights (created by nb2listw()) are used to:

questiontext.text: Spatial weights (created by nb2listw()) are used to:

generalfeedback.text: Spatial weights formalize the spatial relationships defined by the neighbor list. With style = ‘W’ (row-standardized), each neighbor contributes equally to the weighted average. These weights are essential for computing spatial lag, Moran’s I, and LISA.

answer.text: Quantify the strength of spatial relationships between neighboring features for computing spatial statistics

feedback.text: Correct! Spatial weights formalize the spatial relationships defined by the neighbor list. With style = ‘W’ (row-standardized), each neighbor contributes equally to the weighted average. These weights are essential for computing spatial lag, Moran’s I, and LISA.

answer.text: Weight the map colors in a choropleth

feedback.text: Incorrect. Spatial weights formalize the spatial relationships defined by the neighbor list. With style = ‘W’ (row-standardized), each neighbor contributes equally to the weighted average. These weights are essential for computing spatial lag, Moran’s I, and LISA.

answer.text: Reduce the file size of spatial datasets

feedback.text: Incorrect. Spatial weights formalize the spatial relationships defined by the neighbor list. With style = ‘W’ (row-standardized), each neighbor contributes equally to the weighted average. These weights are essential for computing spatial lag, Moran’s I, and LISA.

answer.text: Convert geographic coordinates to projected coordinates

feedback.text: Incorrect. Spatial weights formalize the spatial relationships defined by the neighbor list. With style = ‘W’ (row-standardized), each neighbor contributes equally to the weighted average. These weights are essential for computing spatial lag, Moran’s I, and LISA.

name.text: M4Q04: The spatial lag of a variable is:

questiontext.text: The spatial lag of a variable is:

generalfeedback.text: The spatial lag at location i is the weighted average of the variable’s values at i’s neighbors. It represents ‘what the neighborhood looks like’ and is plotted against the original value in Moran’s scatter plot to visualize spatial autocorrelation.

answer.text: The weighted average of that variable’s values in neighboring areas

feedback.text: Correct! The spatial lag at location i is the weighted average of the variable’s values at i’s neighbors. It represents ‘what the neighborhood looks like’ and is plotted against the original value in Moran’s scatter plot to visualize spatial autocorrelation.

answer.text: The time delay between disease onset in adjacent districts

feedback.text: Incorrect. The spatial lag at location i is the weighted average of the variable’s values at i’s neighbors. It represents ‘what the neighborhood looks like’ and is plotted against the original value in Moran’s scatter plot to visualize spatial autocorrelation.

answer.text: The difference between observed and expected values

feedback.text: Incorrect. The spatial lag at location i is the weighted average of the variable’s values at i’s neighbors. It represents ‘what the neighborhood looks like’ and is plotted against the original value in Moran’s scatter plot to visualize spatial autocorrelation.

answer.text: The residual from a spatial regression model

feedback.text: Incorrect. The spatial lag at location i is the weighted average of the variable’s values at i’s neighbors. It represents ‘what the neighborhood looks like’ and is plotted against the original value in Moran’s scatter plot to visualize spatial autocorrelation.

name.text: M4Q05: In a Moran’s scatter plot, the upper-right quadrant (high value, high spatial la

questiontext.text: In a Moran’s scatter plot, the upper-right quadrant (high value, high spatial lag) represents:

generalfeedback.text: The Moran’s scatter plot has 4 quadrants: upper-right = HH (hot spots), lower-left = LL (cold spots), lower-right = HL (spatial outlier), upper-left = LH (spatial outlier). The slope of the regression line through this plot is Moran’s I.

answer.text: High-High (HH) clusters: areas with high values surrounded by high values

feedback.text: Correct! The Moran’s scatter plot has 4 quadrants: upper-right = HH (hot spots), lower-left = LL (cold spots), lower-right = HL (spatial outlier), upper-left = LH (spatial outlier). The slope of the regression line through this plot is Moran’s I.

answer.text: Low-Low (LL) clusters: areas with low values surrounded by low values

feedback.text: Incorrect. The Moran’s scatter plot has 4 quadrants: upper-right = HH (hot spots), lower-left = LL (cold spots), lower-right = HL (spatial outlier), upper-left = LH (spatial outlier). The slope of the regression line through this plot is Moran’s I.

answer.text: High-Low (HL) outliers: high values surrounded by low values

feedback.text: Incorrect. The Moran’s scatter plot has 4 quadrants: upper-right = HH (hot spots), lower-left = LL (cold spots), lower-right = HL (spatial outlier), upper-left = LH (spatial outlier). The slope of the regression line through this plot is Moran’s I.

answer.text: Low-High (LH) outliers: low values surrounded by high values

feedback.text: Incorrect. The Moran’s scatter plot has 4 quadrants: upper-right = HH (hot spots), lower-left = LL (cold spots), lower-right = HL (spatial outlier), upper-left = LH (spatial outlier). The slope of the regression line through this plot is Moran’s I.

name.text: M4Q06: A Monte Carlo Moran’s I test with nsim = 9999 and p-value = 0.0001 indicates tha

questiontext.text: A Monte Carlo Moran’s I test with nsim = 9999 and p-value = 0.0001 indicates that:

generalfeedback.text: Monte Carlo simulation randomly permutes the data 9,999 times and computes Moran’s I for each permutation. A p-value of 0.0001 means the observed Moran’s I is more extreme than 99.99% of random permutations — very strong evidence against spatial randomness.

answer.text: The observed spatial pattern is extremely unlikely under complete spatial randomness — strong evidence of spatial clustering

feedback.text: Correct! Monte Carlo simulation randomly permutes the data 9,999 times and computes Moran’s I for each permutation. A p-value of 0.0001 means the observed Moran’s I is more extreme than 99.99% of random permutations — very strong evidence against spatial randomness.

answer.text: There is no spatial autocorrelation in the data

feedback.text: Incorrect. Monte Carlo simulation randomly permutes the data 9,999 times and computes Moran’s I for each permutation. A p-value of 0.0001 means the observed Moran’s I is more extreme than 99.99% of random permutations — very strong evidence against spatial randomness.

answer.text: The analysis used too many simulations and should be rerun

feedback.text: Incorrect. Monte Carlo simulation randomly permutes the data 9,999 times and computes Moran’s I for each permutation. A p-value of 0.0001 means the observed Moran’s I is more extreme than 99.99% of random permutations — very strong evidence against spatial randomness.

answer.text: Exactly 0.01% of the study area shows clustering

feedback.text: Incorrect. Monte Carlo simulation randomly permutes the data 9,999 times and computes Moran’s I for each permutation. A p-value of 0.0001 means the observed Moran’s I is more extreme than 99.99% of random permutations — very strong evidence against spatial randomness.

name.text: M4Q07: The difference between Global Moran’s I and Local Moran’s I (LISA) is:

questiontext.text: The difference between Global Moran’s I and Local Moran’s I (LISA) is:

generalfeedback.text: Global Moran’s I answers: ‘Is there spatial clustering overall?’ Local Moran’s I (LISA) answers: ‘WHERE are the clusters, and what TYPE are they (HH, LL, HL, LH)?’ For public health interventions, local analysis is essential to identify target areas.

answer.text: Global gives ONE summary statistic for the entire study area; Local gives a statistic for EACH spatial unit, identifying WHERE clusters occur

feedback.text: Correct! Global Moran’s I answers: ‘Is there spatial clustering overall?’ Local Moran’s I (LISA) answers: ‘WHERE are the clusters, and what TYPE are they (HH, LL, HL, LH)?’ For public health interventions, local analysis is essential to identify target areas.

answer.text: Global uses Queen contiguity while Local uses Rook contiguity

feedback.text: Incorrect. Global Moran’s I answers: ‘Is there spatial clustering overall?’ Local Moran’s I (LISA) answers: ‘WHERE are the clusters, and what TYPE are they (HH, LL, HL, LH)?’ For public health interventions, local analysis is essential to identify target areas.

answer.text: Global is for point data and Local is for polygon data

feedback.text: Incorrect. Global Moran’s I answers: ‘Is there spatial clustering overall?’ Local Moran’s I (LISA) answers: ‘WHERE are the clusters, and what TYPE are they (HH, LL, HL, LH)?’ For public health interventions, local analysis is essential to identify target areas.

answer.text: They always produce the same result

feedback.text: Incorrect. Global Moran’s I answers: ‘Is there spatial clustering overall?’ Local Moran’s I (LISA) answers: ‘WHERE are the clusters, and what TYPE are they (HH, LL, HL, LH)?’ For public health interventions, local analysis is essential to identify target areas.

name.text: M4Q08: In the rgeoda package, which function computes Local Moran’s I statistics?

questiontext.text: In the rgeoda package, which function computes Local Moran’s I statistics?

generalfeedback.text: rgeoda::local_moran() computes LISA statistics for each spatial unit. moran.test() from spdep computes the GLOBAL Moran’s I. lisa_clusters() extracts cluster labels AFTER local_moran() has been computed.

answer.text: local_moran(weights, sf_object[“variable”])

feedback.text: Correct! rgeoda::local_moran() computes LISA statistics for each spatial unit. moran.test() from spdep computes the GLOBAL Moran’s I. lisa_clusters() extracts cluster labels AFTER local_moran() has been computed.

answer.text: moran.test(sf_object$variable, weights)

feedback.text: Incorrect. rgeoda::local_moran() computes LISA statistics for each spatial unit. moran.test() from spdep computes the GLOBAL Moran’s I. lisa_clusters() extracts cluster labels AFTER local_moran() has been computed.

answer.text: lisa_clusters(sf_object, variable)

feedback.text: Incorrect. rgeoda::local_moran() computes LISA statistics for each spatial unit. moran.test() from spdep computes the GLOBAL Moran’s I. lisa_clusters() extracts cluster labels AFTER local_moran() has been computed.

answer.text: queen_weights(sf_object) |> moran()

feedback.text: Incorrect. rgeoda::local_moran() computes LISA statistics for each spatial unit. moran.test() from spdep computes the GLOBAL Moran’s I. lisa_clusters() extracts cluster labels AFTER local_moran() has been computed.

name.text: M4Q09: In a LISA cluster map, a district classified as ‘Low-High’ (LH) represents:

questiontext.text: In a LISA cluster map, a district classified as ‘Low-High’ (LH) represents:

generalfeedback.text: LH spatial outliers are interesting for public health — they represent areas that are ‘bucking the trend’ of their neighborhood. For malnutrition mapping, an LH district might have a successful local intervention worth studying.

answer.text: A district with a low value of the health indicator surrounded by districts with high values (a spatial outlier)

feedback.text: Correct! LH spatial outliers are interesting for public health — they represent areas that are ‘bucking the trend’ of their neighborhood. For malnutrition mapping, an LH district might have a successful local intervention worth studying.

answer.text: A district where values are decreasing from low to high over time

feedback.text: Incorrect. LH spatial outliers are interesting for public health — they represent areas that are ‘bucking the trend’ of their neighborhood. For malnutrition mapping, an LH district might have a successful local intervention worth studying.

answer.text: A district with low population and high disease incidence

feedback.text: Incorrect. LH spatial outliers are interesting for public health — they represent areas that are ‘bucking the trend’ of their neighborhood. For malnutrition mapping, an LH district might have a successful local intervention worth studying.

answer.text: A district where the LISA statistic is not significant

feedback.text: Incorrect. LH spatial outliers are interesting for public health — they represent areas that are ‘bucking the trend’ of their neighborhood. For malnutrition mapping, an LH district might have a successful local intervention worth studying.

name.text: M4Q10: The Standardized Incidence Ratio (SIR) is calculated as:

questiontext.text: The Standardized Incidence Ratio (SIR) is calculated as:

generalfeedback.text: SIR = Observed / Expected. An SIR > 1 indicates more cases than expected (excess burden), SIR = 1 means the observed rate matches the reference, and SIR < 1 indicates fewer cases than expected. It standardizes for population size.

answer.text: Observed number of cases ÷ Expected number of cases (based on a reference population rate)

feedback.text: Correct! SIR = Observed / Expected. An SIR > 1 indicates more cases than expected (excess burden), SIR = 1 means the observed rate matches the reference, and SIR < 1 indicates fewer cases than expected. It standardizes for population size.

answer.text: Number of cases ÷ Total population × 1000

feedback.text: Incorrect. SIR = Observed / Expected. An SIR > 1 indicates more cases than expected (excess burden), SIR = 1 means the observed rate matches the reference, and SIR < 1 indicates fewer cases than expected. It standardizes for population size.

answer.text: Number of deaths ÷ Number of cases

feedback.text: Incorrect. SIR = Observed / Expected. An SIR > 1 indicates more cases than expected (excess burden), SIR = 1 means the observed rate matches the reference, and SIR < 1 indicates fewer cases than expected. It standardizes for population size.

answer.text: Expected cases ÷ Observed cases

feedback.text: Incorrect. SIR = Observed / Expected. An SIR > 1 indicates more cases than expected (excess burden), SIR = 1 means the observed rate matches the reference, and SIR < 1 indicates fewer cases than expected. It standardizes for population size.

name.text: M4Q11: An SMR (Standardized Mortality Ratio) of 1.5 for a district means:

questiontext.text: An SMR (Standardized Mortality Ratio) of 1.5 for a district means:

generalfeedback.text: SMR = 1.5 means Observed/Expected = 1.5, so observed deaths are 1.5 times (or 50% more than) what would be expected if the district had the same rate as the reference population. Values > 1 signal excess mortality.

answer.text: The district has 50% more deaths than expected based on the reference population rate

feedback.text: Correct! SMR = 1.5 means Observed/Expected = 1.5, so observed deaths are 1.5 times (or 50% more than) what would be expected if the district had the same rate as the reference population. Values > 1 signal excess mortality.

answer.text: The district has 1.5 deaths total

feedback.text: Incorrect. SMR = 1.5 means Observed/Expected = 1.5, so observed deaths are 1.5 times (or 50% more than) what would be expected if the district had the same rate as the reference population. Values > 1 signal excess mortality.

answer.text: The mortality rate is 1.5 per 1000 population

feedback.text: Incorrect. SMR = 1.5 means Observed/Expected = 1.5, so observed deaths are 1.5 times (or 50% more than) what would be expected if the district had the same rate as the reference population. Values > 1 signal excess mortality.

answer.text: The district’s mortality is 50% lower than expected

feedback.text: Incorrect. SMR = 1.5 means Observed/Expected = 1.5, so observed deaths are 1.5 times (or 50% more than) what would be expected if the district had the same rate as the reference population. Values > 1 signal excess mortality.

name.text: M4Q12: You find that Global Moran’s I = 0.68 (p < 0.001) for stunting in Indian distric

questiontext.text: You find that Global Moran’s I = 0.68 (p < 0.001) for stunting in Indian districts. This means:

generalfeedback.text: Moran’s I = 0.68 is strongly positive (range: -1 to +1). Combined with p < 0.001, this confirms that stunting rates are spatially clustered: high-stunting districts tend to be near other high-stunting districts, forming spatial hotspots.

answer.text: There is strong positive spatial autocorrelation — districts with similar stunting rates tend to cluster geographically

feedback.text: Correct! Moran’s I = 0.68 is strongly positive (range: -1 to +1). Combined with p < 0.001, this confirms that stunting rates are spatially clustered: high-stunting districts tend to be near other high-stunting districts, forming spatial hotspots.

answer.text: 68% of districts have high stunting rates

feedback.text: Incorrect. Moran’s I = 0.68 is strongly positive (range: -1 to +1). Combined with p < 0.001, this confirms that stunting rates are spatially clustered: high-stunting districts tend to be near other high-stunting districts, forming spatial hotspots.

answer.text: The stunting rate has decreased by 68% over time

feedback.text: Incorrect. Moran’s I = 0.68 is strongly positive (range: -1 to +1). Combined with p < 0.001, this confirms that stunting rates are spatially clustered: high-stunting districts tend to be near other high-stunting districts, forming spatial hotspots.

answer.text: There is negative spatial autocorrelation — high-stunting districts are surrounded by low-stunting districts

feedback.text: Incorrect. Moran’s I = 0.68 is strongly positive (range: -1 to +1). Combined with p < 0.001, this confirms that stunting rates are spatially clustered: high-stunting districts tend to be near other high-stunting districts, forming spatial hotspots.

name.text: M4Q13: In a LISA analysis of malnutrition, you identify a cluster of High-High (HH) dis

questiontext.text: In a LISA analysis of malnutrition, you identify a cluster of High-High (HH) districts in central India. As a public health researcher, you would recommend:

generalfeedback.text: HH clusters represent geographic areas of concentrated burden requiring targeted intervention. The spatial clustering suggests shared underlying determinants (social, environmental, infrastructural) that should be investigated and addressed collectively.

answer.text: Targeted nutritional interventions in these districts, investigating shared determinants (poverty, food insecurity, healthcare access) that explain the spatial cluster

feedback.text: Correct! HH clusters represent geographic areas of concentrated burden requiring targeted intervention. The spatial clustering suggests shared underlying determinants (social, environmental, infrastructural) that should be investigated and addressed collectively.

answer.text: Ignoring the cluster because spatial analysis cannot inform interventions

feedback.text: Incorrect. HH clusters represent geographic areas of concentrated burden requiring targeted intervention. The spatial clustering suggests shared underlying determinants (social, environmental, infrastructural) that should be investigated and addressed collectively.

answer.text: Treating each district independently since spatial patterns are coincidental

feedback.text: Incorrect. HH clusters represent geographic areas of concentrated burden requiring targeted intervention. The spatial clustering suggests shared underlying determinants (social, environmental, infrastructural) that should be investigated and addressed collectively.

answer.text: Collecting more data before taking any action

feedback.text: Incorrect. HH clusters represent geographic areas of concentrated burden requiring targeted intervention. The spatial clustering suggests shared underlying determinants (social, environmental, infrastructural) that should be investigated and addressed collectively.

name.text: M4Q14: In LISA analysis, a district classified as ‘Not Significant’ means there is no e

questiontext.text: In LISA analysis, a district classified as ‘Not Significant’ means there is no evidence of local spatial clustering at that location (at the chosen significance level).

generalfeedback.text: LISA classifies each unit into HH, HL, LH, LL, or Not Significant based on the local Moran’s I statistic AND its p-value. ‘Not Significant’ means the local pattern is consistent with spatial randomness at the given α level (e.g., 0.05).

answer.text: True

feedback.text: Correct! LISA classifies each unit into HH, HL, LH, LL, or Not Significant based on the local Moran’s I statistic AND its p-value. ‘Not Significant’ means the local pattern is consistent with spatial randomness at the given α level (e.g., 0.05).

answer.text: False

feedback.text: Incorrect. LISA classifies each unit into HH, HL, LH, LL, or Not Significant based on the local Moran’s I statistic AND its p-value. ‘Not Significant’ means the local pattern is consistent with spatial randomness at the given α level (e.g., 0.05).

name.text: M4Q15: In the spdep package, which function performs the Monte Carlo significance test

questiontext.text: In the spdep package, which function performs the Monte Carlo significance test for Global Moran’s I?

generalfeedback.text: moran.mc() performs Monte Carlo simulation, randomly permuting values nsim times to generate a null distribution. moran.test() uses the analytical (asymptotic) approach. moran.plot() creates the Moran scatter plot but doesn’t test significance.

answer.text: moran.mc(variable, listw, nsim = 9999)

feedback.text: Correct! moran.mc() performs Monte Carlo simulation, randomly permuting values nsim times to generate a null distribution. moran.test() uses the analytical (asymptotic) approach. moran.plot() creates the Moran scatter plot but doesn’t test significance.

answer.text: moran.test(variable, listw)

feedback.text: Incorrect. moran.mc() performs Monte Carlo simulation, randomly permuting values nsim times to generate a null distribution. moran.test() uses the analytical (asymptotic) approach. moran.plot() creates the Moran scatter plot but doesn’t test significance.

answer.text: local_moran(weights, variable)

feedback.text: Incorrect. moran.mc() performs Monte Carlo simulation, randomly permuting values nsim times to generate a null distribution. moran.test() uses the analytical (asymptotic) approach. moran.plot() creates the Moran scatter plot but doesn’t test significance.

answer.text: moran.plot(variable, listw)

feedback.text: Incorrect. moran.mc() performs Monte Carlo simulation, randomly permuting values nsim times to generate a null distribution. moran.test() uses the analytical (asymptotic) approach. moran.plot() creates the Moran scatter plot but doesn’t test significance.
